I felt that if I said it like this from the start, the meaning wouldn't be conveyed well, so I tried to explain it in more detail. To summarize today's topic: Why is dating so hard? I believe there is a very fundamental problem here. I hope this helps everyone—those who haven't dated yet, those who have, and those who are currently dating—to conceptually understand dating in public a bit better. For the sake of words, everything about the island soba is good. Usually, it can't help but be good. The reason everything is good in this case isn't because everything is actually good, but because I'm not in my right mind. Judgment 3, Shima, patience—none of these are at their usual levels. But if it isn't this hot of science... Dating is a difficult thing to see clearly, so you inevitably have to overdo it a little. They say that's the only way to get a grip. The true essence of what we call dating lies in what comes after that. Why is it that a real relationship begins when I am no longer a special event in life? What are the elements of a healthy relationship at this point? In my opinion, three things would be a stable daily life, high self-esteem, and a composed personality. However, the elements seen in reality are often the exact opposite: an anxious daily life, agile self-esteem, and an intense personality. This is where the irony arises. The qualities that actually allow one to successfully navigate a relationship belong to the former group, yet those who hope for dating and actively jump into the market are far more often the latter. Let's talk about the former first. First of all, for people, dating is a choice; it doesn't matter whether they do it or not. This is because life is enriched even without fruit. A rich life means that daily life is running smoothly. One does not feel a sense of insecurity from being alone. Not rushing into dating allows one to take their time and leisure to find a compatible partner. Even if you meet someone who is a good match, problems will arise, but if you have that composure, you can be much more forgiving toward your partner regarding trivial matters. Because they are not swayed by every little thing, it is fine for them to cause big fights or hurt their partner. They are able to date well because they understand that dating is not a big deal. text: trivial matters. Because they are not swayed by every little thing, it is fine for them to cause big fights or hurt their partner. They are able to date well because they understand that dating is not a big deal. Now let's look at the exact opposite, the latter group. They are obsessed with dating. They have fantasies about it; they expect their lives to change, even if dating is boring. They believe that one person shouldn't brighten their lives. These thoughts grow stronger amidst an unstable daily life. Because they feel lonely and lack certainty when alone, they want someone to always watch over them and constantly prove that they are a good person, a lovable person. Because this craving is so intense, they do not have the luxury to slowly scrutinize a person. They simply start a relationship by grabbing hands while drunk, accepting who comes along and opportunities arise. The probability of finding someone who is a good match is extremely low, yet they wish for a soulmate. For those who lack the time and space to reflect, understand, and compromise on that disappointment, it is difficult to do so; it always easily leads to a big fight. Therefore, because they love dating too much and regard their partner as too beautiful, they are not good at it. This is what I consider to be the fundamental reason. The people easily accessible to us—the "entertainment award winners"—are usually those who are bad at dating. For the sake of the whole, a happy life is not sustained by occasional events. We need to feel the preciousness of a smooth daily routine and find small joys within it.
